problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability): determine the maximum level of single-use/disposable plastic-product waste that can be "safely mitigated" without further environmental damage, derive an achievable minimum waste level and a timeline to reach it, account for regional differences, and address global equity. Team 2008521 (Outstanding) answers this with two custom-built models: a "Maximum Level" mass-balance model for safe plastic throughput, and a "Policy-driven Plastic Impact Index (PII)" comprehensive-evaluation index spanning ecosystem/economy/society, then extends PII over time into a CICI index and uses a GE/McKinsey matrix for the equity sub-problem.
models_used: Maximum Level mass-balance model: M = R1 + R2 + R3, splitting annual plastic waste into industrial recycling (R1=alpha1*AP), incineration (R2=alpha2*AP), and environmental discard/degradation (R3 = (76%*gamma1 + 3%*gamma2)*WIE), with alpha1/alpha2/alpha3 = 9%/12%/79% and soil/ocean degradation timescales gamma1^-1=800yr, gamma2^-1=400yr; yields max safe level ~96.77 million tons/yr
Policy-driven Plastic Impact Index (PII): a three-tier comprehensive evaluation index, PII=(ED1+ED2+SD)/3, scored out of 100, higher = worse

Ecosystem Degree ED1 = (Air+Water+Soil)/3, each a weighted sum over 7 plastic-use industries (Packaging, Textiles, Consumer&Institutional, Transportation, Building&Construction, Electrical/Electronic, Others) using coefficient pairs (w1ia,w2ia for CO2 + noxious gas; w1iw,w2iw for water contamination + health; w1is,w2is for soil contamination + health)

Economy Degree ED2 = 100*(Plastic_loss - Substitute_earn)/Plastic_total, where Plastic_loss = sum w1i*Value_i and Substitute_earn = sum w2i*w1i*Value_i over the 7 industries (substitutability differs by industry)
Society Degree SD = Reliance - Improvement, with Reliance=f_re(GDP) and Improvement=f_im(GDP) both modeled as inverted-U curves vs GDP per capita

CICI (Comprehensive Impact Coefficient Index): time-extended PII with a 3-year decaying-memory recurrence CICI(t) = F(t, production, recovery) + sum_{i=1..3}(1 - i/3)*CICI(t-i), F(t)=ED1(t)+SD(t); used to find minimum achievable level (28.1 kg/capita/yr at 40% recycling, CICI~45, ~15 years out)

GE (McKinsey) Matrix for the equity sub-problem: maps each continent on (GDP development level, CICI impact level) grids, defines Sustainable Development Coefficient SDC as distance from optimum point (1,1), and measures global fairness by the variance of SDC across continents (0.85 -> 0.74 after 5-year intervention simulation)
Control-variate / trial-and-error optimization to find optimal per-capita production minimizing PII/CICI under fixed recycling rate
Sensitivity analysis varying gamma1, gamma2, AP (model 1) and per-industry value/production shares (model 2)
key_techniques: Two complementary models answering different facets: a physical mass-balance for the 'ceiling' (max safe throughput) and a normalized 0-100 evaluation index for the 'severity/optimization' questions

Comprehensive evaluation index construction: nested averaging of sub-indices (Air/Water/Soil -> ED1; ED1/ED2/SD -> PII), each grounded in a 7-industry decomposition so the abstract score traces back to real plastic-use sectors

Embedding policy as a perturbation of model inputs rather than a new model: policies (import ban, extra tax) modeled by sweeping an 'execution rate'/'extra rate' and re-running PII, revealing region-specific inflection points (~55% execution) that differentiate East-Asia (import-processing) vs Europe (export) economies
Temporal memory via a decaying 3-year recurrence in CICI to capture that this year's damage persists into future years (lagged environmental impact)

Cross-domain analogy: borrowing the GE/McKinsey business matrix (industry attractiveness x competitive strength) and reinterpreting axes as (GDP development x plastic impact) to score country sustainability
Defining fairness operationally as variance of SDC across continents, turning an abstract 'equity' ask into a single minimizable scalar
Concrete numeric grounding using authoritative sources (Geyer et al. Science Advances 2017, Jambeck et al. Science 2015) for AP=407 Mt, treatment splits, degradation timescales
Case-study contrast (East-Asia vs Europe) to demonstrate the model captures structural economic differences, not just magnitudes
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness, structure, and interpretability rather than mathematical sophistication. It answers every part of the prompt in order (max level -> regional minima -> timeline -> equity -> memo) with a clear thread connecting the models: the same PII index is reused and progressively extended (PII -> CICI -> SDC), so the paper reads as one coherent framework rather than four disconnected tasks. Every abstract score is decomposed back to the 7 real plastic-use industries and grounded in named, citable data sources, which signals scientific credibility. The policy analysis is genuinely insightful: by sweeping execution rates it surfaces a counterintuitive, defensible finding (an import ban eventually HURTS import-dependent East-Asia while helping export-heavy Europe), demonstrating the model produces non-trivial, decision-relevant conclusions. It quantifies fairness as a single number (SDC variance) and shows measurable improvement under intervention. The required memo is delivered cleanly with an explicit minimum level (~45 index, production down two-thirds, recovery 20%->40%, reachable in 15 years) plus named accelerating/obstructing circumstances. Judges reward a model that is intuitive, traceable, fully responsive, and ends with actionable recommendations -- this paper does all four."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet enumerates each task's model and headline number up front (96.77 Mt max; PII 58/32/78 for global/Europe/East-Asia; optimal 28.1 kg/capita at 40% recycling, CICI 45 in 15 yrs) -- a judge can grasp the whole solution in one read. The Assumptions section explicitly justifies each simplification (data reliability, country-as-unit, continent-level policy). Model exposition flows top-down: define M, then each R term gets its own subsection with mechanism (mechanical/chemical recycling, incineration toxicity, soil/ocean degradation) before the formula. The PII section uses a clean three-tier visual schematic (Figure 2) tying Economy/Society/Ecology to sub-factors. The East-Asia vs Europe case study with side-by-side stacked bars makes the structural contrast legible. Sensitivity analysis is split per-model and identifies the dominant drivers (AP for model 1, Packaging industry for model 2). The memo is professionally formatted (TO/FROM/DATE/RE) and stays decision-focused with concrete numbers and a beneficial/obstructive-circumstances breakdown."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy reliance on hand-set coefficient tables (the w1ia...w2is weight matrix in Table 1) with no stated sourcing or calibration method -- the weights appear assumed/tuned, undermining the 'objective, subjective-factors-excluded' claim the paper makes in Strengths

Internal numeric inconsistencies: the pie chart gives R1=36.63, R2=48.84, R3=11.3 summing to ~96.77, but the text earlier states alpha1=9% (recycle) < alpha2=12% (incinerate) yet R1 is treated as industrial recycling -- labeling/quantities are muddled; East-Asia optimal is stated as both 37.09 and 38.07 kg/capita and PII min as both 29.46 and 29.64

Conceptual stretch in the 'maximum level' definition: equating degradation capacity + recycling + incineration with a 'safe' production ceiling treats incineration (which the paper itself calls highly toxic, dioxin-producing) as benign mitigation, which contradicts the 'no further environmental damage' criterion

PII/CICI units and scaling are opaque: scores 'out of 100' but the weighted sums over industries have no normalization shown, so it's unclear how raw sums map to 0-100; figures (e.g. global PII 58 vs optimal 42.05) are asserted from plots without the underlying functional forms F(t), f_re, f_im ever being specified
The CICI 3-year memory recurrence is plausible but its kernel (1 - i/3) and the 15-year horizon are chosen by assertion, not derived or validated

GE-matrix equity model normalizes GDP and CICI to (0,1) and computes SDC as Euclidean distance to (1,1) without justifying equal weighting of the two axes; SDC 'variance' as a fairness metric is sensitive to that arbitrary normalization
Numerous English/grammar and typo issues (PPI vs PII, '??' placeholder symbols left in equations) that, while not fatal, reflect rushed editing
Strong cooperation assumption: equity-intervention results assume all regions actively comply (acknowledged in Weaknesses but it materially drives the optimistic SDC improvement)
reusable_patterns: Two-model split for an open sustainability prompt: a physical/mass-balance model for the hard 'capacity/ceiling' question + a normalized comprehensive-evaluation index for the soft 'severity/optimization' question
Build one index and extend it across tasks instead of inventing a new model per task: PII (static) -> CICI (time-lagged) -> SDC (equity), giving the paper a single narrative spine
Decompose any aggregate index back to a fixed, real-world category set (here 7 plastic-use industries) so every score is traceable and every sub-question can reuse the same breakdown
Model policy as a parameter sweep over model inputs (execution rate / tax rate) and look for inflection points -- a cheap way to produce region-specific, counterintuitive, decision-relevant findings
Encode temporal persistence with a short decaying-memory recurrence X(t)=F(t)+sum w_i*X(t-i) when this period's state affects future periods
Operationalize a vague qualitative ask ('equity'/'fairness') as a single scalar to minimize -- here variance of a per-region coefficient
Repurpose a known business/management framework (GE/McKinsey 2x2 matrix) by remapping its axes to domain variables and using distance-to-ideal-corner as a composite score
Anchor every key parameter to a named authoritative citation (Geyer/Jambeck Science papers) and surface the headline numbers in the summary sheet
Deliver the required memo in real business format and close with explicit accelerating vs obstructing factors -- concrete, actionable, judge-friendly
Split sensitivity analysis per model and name the single dominant driver of each to demonstrate you understand which assumptions matter
